segments.s021-g25: به + خونش + بپرورد: به here means 'by/with' (instrumental), خونش is 'his blood' (possessive -ش), and بپرورد is a 3rd-person subjunctive/optative form (ب‑ + پرورد) used in classical Persian to express 'may he raise/nurture' or an exhortation. بر سان شیر is a prepositional simile 'in the manner of / like a lion' (بر + سان + noun).
segments.s021-g26: بدان is here composed of ب (prefix) + دان; together it functions adverbially ('by/with that' / 'thereby') linking to the previous clause. تا introduces purpose ('so that'), کند is 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'may/might make', پادشا را is 'the king' with را marking the definite object, and دلیر is an object/complement 'brave' (i.e. 'make the king brave').
segments.s021-g27: سخن هر چه is a free relative meaning 'whatever speech/words', گویدش is 3rd-person singular verb 'he says/ tells it to him' (the clitic ‑ش = 'it/him'), and فرمان کند means 'he makes [it] a command' (کند = 'makes/does'); the clause states that any utterance of his becomes an order.
segments.s021-g28: به فرمان او is an agentive/instrumental phrase 'by his command' (به = 'by/through'), دل گروگان کند is literal word order 'takes the heart hostage' where دل is the object 'heart', گروگان is 'hostage/pledge' and کند = 'does/takes' (3rd person).
segments.s021-g29: خورش زرده خایه is a noun sequence describing the food (a stew/dish made from testicle‑yolk); Persian often strings nouns like this without additional case marking. دادش is past tense 'he gave it/him' (داد + enclitic ‑ش = 'to him/it'), and نخست is an adverb 'first/at first'.
segments.s021-g30: بدان here functions 'by/with that' (linking back to the previous action). داشتش is past 'he kept/held him' (داشت + ‑ش), یک زمان is a duration 'for a time/for a while', and تندرست is a predicative adjective 'healthy' (i.e. 'kept him healthy for a while').
segments.s021-g31: بخورد here is a narrative verb 'he ate' (classical usage with ب‑ + root) followed by و 'and'. بر او آفرین کرد is 'he made praise upon him' = 'he praised him', where آفرین functions as a noun/exclamation 'praise/bravo' and کرد is the past verb 'did/made'. سخت modifies کرد adverbially 'strongly/heartily'.
segments.s021-g32: مزه یافت is 'he found the taste' (یافت = 'found/experienced'), خواندش here means 'he called/named him' (خواند + enclitic ‑ش = 'him'), ورا is a dative/locative 'to him/there', and نیک‌بخت is a substantive/nickname 'fortunate one' used as the name he gives (i.e. 'named him Nīk‑bakht').
segments.s021-g33: 'چنین' is an adverb meaning 'thus' placed before the verb; 'گفت' is the past tense 'said' and the sequence 'چنین گفت ابلیس نیرنگ‌ساز' is the typical Persian order 'thus said [subject] [epithet]'. 'نیرنگ‌ساز' is an epithet (post‑nominal modifier) describing ابلیس.
segments.s021-g34: 'که' here introduces the quoted speech ('that' = 'saying that'); 'شادان' is an adjective used adverbially ('joyful' / 'rejoicing'); 'زی‌ای' is the poetic/imparative form 'زی' (live!) with the vocative particle 'ای' attached to address someone directly; 'شاه' is the vocative 'O king' and 'گردنفراز' is a descriptive epithet 'high‑necked / haughty' modifying شاه. Word order places the imperative and vocative next to each other, then the addressed noun with its epithet.
segments.s021-g35: 'که' again introduces reported speech or purpose ('that'); 'فردات' here is an archaic/poetic temporal/temporalizing word (contextually 'for tomorrow' / 'on the morrow') — the form is older than modern 'فردا' and is somewhat irregular; 'از آن گونه' = 'from that kind / of that sort'; 'سازم' is first person present/optative used here with future sense 'I will make' or 'I shall make'; 'خورش' is the direct object 'a stew / cooked dish'.
segments.s021-g36: 'کز' = 'که از' = 'from which / of which' (a relative/linking particle) referring back to 'خورش'; 'او' = 'it' (third‑person pronoun referring to that stew); 'باشدت' is an archaic form with the enclitic '-ت' (= 'to you'), here optative/indicative meaning 'it will be to you' or 'may it be for you'; 'سربه‌سر' is an adverb/adjective 'entirely/altogether'; 'پرورش' denotes 'nurture/nourishment' (here used of the stew as providing sustenance). The whole clause means 'which will be, for you, wholly a nourishment.'
segments.s021-g37: 'برفت' is an archaic past form 'he went away' (classical variant of 'برفت'/'رفت'); 'و' = 'and'; 'همه شب' = 'the whole night / all night'; 'سگالش گرفت' is an idiomatic/minor/rare compound: سگالش (a rare noun meaning worry/anxiety/solicitude) plus گرفت ('took/seized'), so together 'he was seized by anxiety' or 'he fretted'. Grammatically, سگالش is the object/cause with گرفت as the verb 'to seize'.
segments.s021-g38: 'که' links this subordinate clause to گرفت (introducing what he worried about): 'that / (wondering) that'; 'فردا' = 'tomorrow'; 'ز' is the poetic form of 'از' = 'from/of'; 'خوردن' is the verbal noun 'eating' (object of 'از' — 'what to eat'); 'چه' is the interrogative 'what'; 'سازد' is present/subjunctive (used with future or deliberative sense) 'he will/do/make' or 'he should make'; 'شگفت' is an adjectival/parenthetical 'wondrous/astonishing', modifying the action 'what he would make' (i.e. 'what wondrous thing he might prepare to eat'). Word order is typical Persian: complementizer + temporal + source + infinitive + interrogative + verb + qualifier.
segments.s021-g39: 'خورش‌ها' is the plural 'stews / dishes'; 'ز' = 'from'; 'کبک' is the ingredient 'partridge'; 'و' = 'and'; 'تذرو' appears as a noun (likely a bird or ingredient name here — rare/uncertain); 'سپید' is an adjective 'white' post‑nominally modifying تذرو. The phrase lists the ingredients of the stews: 'stews from partridge and the white tazru.'
segments.s021-g40: 'بسازید' in this epic context functions as a past narrative form 'they made / they prepared' (though identical in shape to a modern imperative, context and parallel verbs make it past here); 'و' = 'and'; 'آمد' = 'came' (past); 'دلی' = 'a heart' (here a noun meaning 'a feeling/heart'); 'پر' = 'full (of)'; 'امید' = 'hope' — 'and a heart full of hope came' i.e. 'he became hopeful' or 'hope filled his heart.'
segments.s021-g49: چو is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when' (archaic/poetic). ضحاک is the proper name (subject). دست اندر آورد is a light-verb expression: دست (hand) + اندر (in) + آورد (brought/put) = 'put (his) hand in'. و links the next verb; خورد is the past tense 'ate'. The whole segment gives the temporal clause 'When Zahhak put his hand in and ate...'.
segments.s021-g50: شگفت آمدش is an impersonal/experiential phrase meaning 'it was astonishing to him' (شگفت as 'astonishment/wonder', آمد + -ش = 'came to him'). زان = 'from/at that' (referring back to the action just described). هشیوار modifies مرد: 'the alert/wise man'. The segment states the effect of the previous clause: the man was astonished by that.
segments.s021-g51: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him/unto him', گفت = 'said'); بنگر is an imperative/hortative 'look' or 'see' introducing the speech; که introduces the content clause that follows. از آرزوی = 'from/of desire' (از marking the source/subject of the wished thing). The segment sets up reported speech: 'he said to him, "see/consider that regarding your desire..."'.
segments.s021-g52: چه خواهی = 'what do you want' (چه 'what' + خواهی second‑person present/future 'you will/want'); بگو is the imperative 'say' (command to speak); با من = 'to me' (with me/as for me), and ای نیک‌خوی is a vocative 'O good‑natured one' addressing the listener. The clause is a direct request: 'tell me what you want, O kind one.'
segments.s021-g53: خورشگر is the speaker ('the cook'); بدو = 'to him' (dative pronoun), not the imperative 'run'; گفت is the verb 'said' (past) but the provided sense list does not include the verb sense (so sense index is -1). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone, and پادشا is 'king'.
segments.s021-g54: همیشه 'always' is an adverb; the clause that follows is elliptical (poetry commonly omits the copula or imperative auxiliary), so this reads as a wish/imperative ('May you always ...'). بزی is adjectival/epithetic ('goat‑like' or 'as a goat') used playfully here; شاد 'happy/merry' and فرمانروا 'ruler/sovereign' are predicates joined by و. The sense is a blessing/wish: 'May you always be a merry [goat‑like] sovereign.'
segments.s021-g55: مرا دل is an older/paragogic ordering where مرا functions like a possessive/topic ('my') attached to دل ('heart') instead of the modern دلِ من; سراسر 'throughout' modifies دل; پر از مهر تو = 'full of your love'; the clause closes with the copula است 'is'. In short: 'My heart is throughout full of your love.'
segments.s021-g56: همه 'all' introduces the noun phrase توشه جانم ('the provisions/tokens of my life') where جانم = 'my life/soul' (the possessive suffix is shown in the tokenization). از چهر تو است = 'are from your face' (i.e. originate with/are caused by your appearance or presence). The clause is a copular statement: 'All the provisions of my life are from your face.'
segments.s021-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a clause. ابلیس is the subject, پیوسته is an adverb 'continually/constantly' modifying the verb, and دید is the past tense verb 'saw/beheld'; آن سخن is the direct object 'that speech'. The word order is a typical classical Persian clause: conjunction + subject + adverb + verb + object.
segments.s021-g2: This is a compact, somewhat poetic object-verb phrase: یکی بند بد را = 'one/ a certain evil stanza' ( numerative + noun + adjective + object marker را ), نو qualifies the stanza as 'new', and افگند is the past of افکندن 'cast/put/composed'. Word order is literary (adjectives and modifiers may follow); بن at the end is archaic/poetic and here does not correspond to any of the literal dictionary senses given (it functions as a discourse/poetic particle), so it is left unassigned.
segments.s021-g3: بدو here is the prepositional 'to him' introducing reported speech. گفت‌گر functions as the speech introducer ('the speaker said' / 'he said'), followed by the protasis of a conditional: سوی من تافتی = 'if you turn/face toward me' (تافتی is the 2sg past form used in classical Persian conditional sense 'you would/should turn'). The clause is elliptical: گفت‌گر introduces the quoted conditional that follows.
segments.s021-g4: ز گیتی = 'from the world' (ز = from, گیتی = the world). همه کام دل is a genitival phrase 'all the desires of the heart' (همه = all; کام = desire; دل = heart). یافتی is the 2nd-person past form 'you would find/obtain' (here used as the apodosis to the conditional introduced earlier). Together they express the apodosis: 'you would obtain all your heart's desires from the world.'
segments.s021-g5: اگر introduces a conditional 'if'. همچنین نیز is an emphatic/clarifying adverbial 'in this way / likewise / also' (both adverbs add emphasis), پیمان کنی is the 2nd-person present/subjunctive 'you make the pact'—classical Persian uses this verbal form in conditional protases.
segments.s021-g6: نپیچی is the negative present/subjunctive form 'you do not turn/you do not deviate' (ن- prefix negates پیچیدن 'to turn/veer'). ز گفتار و فرمان کنی = 'from the speech/words and (you) do the command' — ز marks 'from', گفتار = 'speech/words', و links to فرمان 'command', and کنی (again subjunctive/polite present) means 'you do/obey'. The pair expresses admonition: 'do not turn away from the words, and do the command.'
segments.s021-g7: جهان سربه‌سر is an adverbial/nominal phrase 'the whole world / throughout the world' (سربه‌سر = entirely). پادشاهی تو راست is a nominal equative: 'the kingship/kingdom is yours' (پادشاهی = kingdom, تو = you/your, راست used predicatively 'is/belongs/true'). The phrase states possession/sovereignty over the entire world.
segments.s021-g8: This is a simple coordinate list of subjects: دد و مردم و مرغ و ماهی = 'enemies and people and birds and fish' linked by و. The final تو راست repeats the predicative 'are yours' (تو = you/your; راست = is/are true/belong). Grammatically the sequence lists what is under the addressee's rule/possession.
segments.s021-g17: چو (when) + بشنید (he heard) begins a temporal clause; ضحاک is the subject (Zahhak). بنواختش is verb+object pronoun (he struck him/it) — بنواخت is past tense/preterite and -ش refers to a previously mentioned person/thing. Word order is literary: temporal particle + verb + subject + verb-with-pronoun.
segments.s021-g18: ز بهر خورش جایگه ساختش is a consecutive clause: ز بهر = 'for/because of' (here 'for the sake of'), خورش = 'meal/stew', جایگه ساختش = 'he made (ساخت) a place/seat (جایگه) for him' with -ش again the object pronoun. ساخت is past-tense 'made/built'; the clause explains the purpose for which he acted.
segments.s021-g19: This noun phrase strings together genitives: کلید (key) of خورش‌خانه (the food-house/kitchen) and پادشا (the king) modifies the whole as 'the key of the king's kitchen.' Classical Persian often stacks nouns without additional linking particles.
segments.s021-g20: بدو داد = 'gave to him' (داد is past 'gave', بدو = 'to him/unto it'); دستور فرمانروا = 'by the ruler's order/command' where دستور means 'order' and فرمانروا ('ruler') specifies whose command it was. Word order is ergative/poetic: indirect object (بدو) before verb.
segments.s021-g21: فراوان نبود is a negated predicate placed before the noun phrase (inverted order): 'was not plentiful'. آن زمان پرورش is the time‑phrase + noun ('the provision/supply of that time') serving as the subject/topic. Together: 'There was not abundant provision at that time.'
segments.s021-g22: که introduces the result/clause ('so that/so'): the clause says what happened for lack of provision. کمتر بد is a comparative phrase meaning 'the lesser/poorer (one)' (literally 'less bad'); از خوردنی‌ها marks source/material 'from the edibles/foods'; خورش (placed at the end) is the noun 'stew' or 'cooked dish' that was made from those inferior foods.
segments.s021-g23: ز is the poetic form of از ('from'); هر گوشت = 'each/every meat' (i.e. every sort of meat). The sequence از مرغ و از چارپای repeats the preposition for parallel items ('from birds and from quadrupeds') and specifies the sources of the meat.
segments.s021-g24: خورشگر is the subject ('the cook'). بیاورد is a narrative/habitual past form rendered here as 'would bring' or 'brought' (he habitually brought or produced). یک‌یک is a distributive adverb 'one by one'; به جای literally 'to the place/into position' and here means 'in turn / to the table / in their place' — i.e. the cook brought the stews one by one to serve.
segments.s021-g9: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when' introducing a temporal clause. این کرده شد is a perfect-participial clause: این (this) + کرده (done, participle) + شد (auxiliary 'to be' in past), so 'when this was done'. ساز دیگر گرفت is the main clause: ساز (here 'device/means') + دیگر ('another') + گرفت (simple past 'took/adopted'); the subject is omitted (he) as typical in Persian.
segments.s021-g10: یکی functions as an indefiner 'one/a single' and introduces the result clause. چاره کرد is a simple past 'made/contrived a remedy/solution' (چاره = solution, کرد = did/made). از شگفتی شگفت is a poetic intensifying phrase: از + noun شگفتی ('marvel/astonishment') + adjective شگفت ('wondrous'), giving the sense 'a marvel among marvels' or 'astonishingly wondrous'.
segments.s021-g11: جوانی is the subject 'a youth'. برآراست is past tense (3rd sing.) of برآراستن 'to adorn/array/put on', so 'arrayed himself/appeared adorned'. از خویشتن is a prepositional phrase 'from/of himself' meaning 'of his own person' or 'himself' (emphatic), modifying how he presented himself.
segments.s021-g12: This is a coordinated nominal string of qualities. سخنگوی (a speaker/spokesman) stands alone as a quality; و is 'and'; بینا دل is an adjective + noun where بینا ('discerning/clear-sighted') qualifies دل ('heart/mind') — idiomatically 'with discerning heart' or 'clear-sighted in heart'; و رایزن adds 'and an adviser' (رایزن = counselor). Word order is adjective + noun (بینا دل) which is common in poetry.
segments.s021-g13: همیدون is a proper name (the subject). به ضحاک is the prepositional object 'toward Zahhak' (به = to/toward). بنهاد is past of نهادن with a prefixed ب- (بنهاد) meaning 'set/placed' and here idiomatically 'he turned' (بنهادِ روی = 'set his face'); روی is the direct object 'face'. The clause literally 'Hamidun set his face toward Zahhak' = 'Hamidun turned to Zahhak.'
segments.s021-g14: نبودش is a past negation with the clitic -ش ('there was not for him' / 'he lacked'). به جز is the fixed phrase 'except'. آفرین گفت و گوی groups 'آفرین' (an acclamation/praise) with گفت و گوی ('speech/words, conversation'); together the phrase means 'he had nothing but praise and words' — i.e. his only offerings were praise and talk. Note گفت و گوی is a nominal compound 'talk and saying'.
segments.s021-g15: بدو is the prepositional phrase 'to him' (بـ + او → بدو) marking the indirect object. گفت is the reporting verb 'said' (here used as speech-verb); the provided sense list does not include the verbal sense, so no exact sense index is available. اگر introduces the conditional 'if'. شاه را uses the definite marker را with شاه (king) as the item judged; در خورم is the predicative idiom 'to be fitting/acceptable' with خورم being first-person singular ending (-م) on an older/formal stem (در خور = 'worthy of, fitting for'), so 'if I am acceptable to the king.'
segments.s021-g16: یکی here functions as an indefinite 'one/a certain'. نامور and پاک are attributive adjectives 'renowned' and 'pure' that qualify the following predicate. خوالیگرم is an archaic/obscure form (lemma given as خوالیگر) ending in -م (1st person), so grammatically it is a first-person present/future predicate tied to the preceding conditional — 'I will be / I will make / I will become' — but its exact lexical meaning is uncertain in this text, so it remains marked as an obscure term.
segments.s021-g89: مگر functions as a conditional/contrastive particle here ("unless"/"perhaps"); تا introduces purpose/result ("so that"), not temporal "until". یکی is an indefinite subject "someone." چاره is the noun "remedy/plan," and سازد is a present/subjunctive‑flavored verb "may/might make/devise" (classical Persian uses this form for an optative sense). نهان follows the verb as an adverb "secretly," modifying سازد.
segments.s021-g90: که introduces a subordinate/result clause ("so that/that"). پردخته is an older past‑participle/adjectival form ("made, composed, set/fashioned"). گردد is the classical optative/passive auxiliary "become / be made" (here giving a potential/passive meaning). ز + مردم جهان indicates agent/source in poetic diction ("by/from the people of the world" or more loosely "among the people of the world").
segments.s021-g41: This is a temporal subordinate clause introduced by چون (here 'when'). شه تازیان is a nominal phrase 'the king of the Arabs' (شه = 'king' + تازیان = 'Arabs'). به نان دست برد is an idiomatic expression: به + noun ('to/at the bread') + دست برد (literally 'laid hand', i.e. 'laid hands on / took to') — the verb برد is the past form, completing the clause.
segments.s021-g42: سر کم خرد is a noun phrase meaning 'the small‑minded chief/head' (سر = 'chief/head'; کم خرد = 'of little wisdom'). مهر is the thing given ('seal'/'stamp' here) and او را is the pronoun marked by را referring back to that person; سپرد is the past tense of سپردن 'to entrust/hand over'. The order places the recipient phrase (سر کم خرد) before the clause 'مهر او را سپرد' ('he handed the seal to him').
segments.s021-g43: سیم روز (here understood as a time phrase 'on the third day') modifies خوان ('the table/banquet'). خوان را uses the object marker را to mark the table as what is being arranged; به introduces the instruments/contents 'with birds and lambs' (i.e., he adorned the table with fowl and lamb).
segments.s021-g44: بیاراستش is a past transitive verb with the enclitic pronoun -ش ('it/him') attached, so 'he arrayed/arranged it.' گونه گون is a reduplicated adjective meaning 'of various kinds' and یک‌سره is an adverb meaning 'wholly/entirely' — together they qualify how the table was arranged.
segments.s021-g45: به‌روز چهارم is a time expression 'on the fourth day' (به‌روز = 'of the day' here). چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when', and بنهاد (past of نهادن) means 'he placed/put' — so چو بنهاد خوان = 'when he set the table.' Word order places the time phrase first and the subordinate clause after it.
segments.s021-g46: خورش ساخت is a simple past clause 'he made/constructed a stew/dish' (خورش = 'stew/dish', ساخت = 'made'). از پشت گاو جوان is a prepositional phrase 'from the back of a young ox' specifying the source/cut used for the stew (از = 'from', پشت = 'back', گاو جوان = 'a young ox').
segments.s021-g47: بدو اندرون is a short locative/benefactive phrase: بدو ('to it'/'unto it') + اندرون ('inside'), so 'into it, inside' — here indicating ingredients added into the stew. زعفران و گلاب list the added ingredients 'saffron and rosewater' (و = 'and').
segments.s021-g48: همان qualifies the following items ('that same' / 'likewise'); سالخورده describes the wine as 'aged' (literally 'year‑eaten', i.e. old/vintaged). می‌و here names a liquid/fruit product (classical usage in context is wine); مشک ناب is a noun phrase 'pure musk' (مشک = 'musk', ناب = 'pure'). Together they form a coordinated list of fragrant/additive ingredients.
segments.s021-g57: یکی here functions as 'a certain' or 'some' modifying حاجتستم; حاجتستم is the first-person present construction 'I am in need' (حاجت + -ستم), so the whole phrase means 'I have a certain need to/for being near the king.' Word order places the quantifier/qualifier before the verb phrase; به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase ('to/near the king').
segments.s021-g58: گرچه introduces a concessive clause ('although'). مرا نیست is an archaic/inverted way to say 'it is not to me' → 'I do not have it' (مرا is the dative/object clitic before the copula/existential), with نیست = 'is not.' این پایگاه is the item lacking ('this standing/position'); the clause concedes the speaker lacks that status.
segments.s021-g59: که introduces a subordinate/content clause ('that'); فرمان دهد is 'gives/orders' with دهد in the present/subjunctive register (reporting the king's ordering). تا introduces purpose/result ('so that') and is followed by the noun phrase سر کتف اوی ('the top/head of his shoulder'), where اوی is the archaic possessive 'his.' The noun phrase is the intended object of the verb in the following clause.
segments.s021-g60: ببوسم is the first-person singular present/subjunctive of بوسیدن ('that I kiss' / 'may I kiss') used after تا to express purpose. بدو is the dative 'to him.' The phrase بر نهم (here written in the line though نهم is not separately listed in the word array) is the first-person present/subjunctive of نهادن with the prefix بر- meaning 'I place/lay upon'; چشم و روی ('eyes and face') is the object placed upon him. The two verbal actions (kiss; place eyes/face) are coordinated under the same subject 'I.'
segments.s021-g61: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when.' ضحاک is the subject name and بشنید is the simple past 'he heard.' گفتار اوی is the object phrase 'his words/speech' (گفتار + possessive اوی). The clause describes Zahhak's act of hearing.
segments.s021-g62: نهانی here is an adjective/adverb 'secret, hidden' modifying بازار اوی ('his dealings/market'). ندانست is the past negative of دانستن 'he did not know/recognize/ perceive.' The word order is somewhat inverted/poetic: 'he did not know (or perceive) his secret market/dealings.'
segments.s021-g63: بدو گفت is the common inverted order 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him', گفت = 'said'). دارم من is 'I have' with the pronoun من used for emphasis, and این کام تو = 'this desire of yours' (این = 'this', کام = 'desire', تو = 'you/your'). The clause is direct speech quoted by 'he said to him.'
segments.s021-g64: بلندی is the noun 'loftiness/height/renown' (subject) and بگیرد is third-person singular present/subjunctive or modal 'may seize / will take' (here: 'will be gained/taken'). از این نام تو is a prepositional phrase 'from this name of yours' with نام meaning 'name' as 'reputation/fame' and تو the possessive 'your.' Together: 'Loftiness will be taken from this name of yours' → 'great renown will spring from your name.'
segments.s021-g65: بفرمود تا is a classical periphrasis meaning “he commanded that…” (بفرمود = honorific/past ‘he said/ordered’). تا introduces a subordinate clause/command, and the following noun phrase دیو چون جفت او is the logical subject of that subordinate clause: دیو = 'the demon', چون + جفت او = 'like his mate' (چون = 'like', جفت = 'mate/pair', او = 'he').
segments.s021-g66: همی is an older epic particle that with past verbs conveys a continuous or iterative past (‘kept on / then’); بوسه داد is the verbal phrase ‘gave a kiss’ (بوسه = kiss; داد = gave). از بر is a poetic prepositional combination meaning 'from/upon' or 'on', and سفتِ او (‘his firm/rigid [chest/part]’) is the noun phrase governed by that preposition (سفت = firm/hard used adjectivally, او = his).
segments.s021-g67: ببوسید is the simple past ‘he kissed’. و here functions as a sequential connector 'and/then' linking two actions. شد is the past of شدن ‘became / came to be’; combined with the predicate that follows it, it yields ‘he became/was …’. بر زمین is the locative 'on/onto the ground', and ناپدید is a predicate adjective 'vanished/ disappeared' that completes شدن: 'he vanished (became unseen) onto the ground.'
segments.s021-g68: کس here functions negatively with the verb (contextually 'no one'); اندر جهان is the prepositional phrase 'in the world'. این شگفتی is the demonstrative + noun 'this wonder/marvel' as the thing referenced. ندید is the past form of دید with the negative prefix ن- in classical usage ('did not see' / 'has never seen'), so the whole clause means 'No one in the world has seen this wonder.'
segments.s021-g69: دو مار سیه is a noun phrase 'two black snakes' (مار = snake, سیه = black/dark; in older Persian the adjective often follows or accompanies the noun). از دو کتفش is the source phrase 'from his two shoulders' (کتفش = 'his shoulders' with the possessive -ش). برست is an archaic past meaning 'burst forth / sprang up' (i.e. the snakes emerged).
segments.s021-g70: غمی گشت is an inchoative expression: غمی = 'sorrow/grief' and گشت (from گشتن) = 'became / came about', so 'sorrow came upon him'. و again links to the next clause. از هر سویی چاره جست is an idiomatic sequence: از هر سویی = 'from every direction', چاره = 'a remedy/means', and جست (past of جستن) = 'sought', so 'he sought a remedy from every side.'
segments.s021-g71: سرانجام is an adverb 'finally/at last'. ببرید is a past transitive form (here 'they cut off / they removed' or more generally 'they took away')—an older/polite past form of بردن used in narrative. هر دو ز introduces what was removed: 'both from …' (ز = poetic 'from') and کفت here functions as the source noun (contextually 'shoulder'—the text uses a variant form).
segments.s021-g72: سزد‌گر is an evaluative/conditional device 'if it befit / if one deems it proper' (literally 'one who deems fit' used adverbially). بمانی is the present/subjunctive form 'you remain / you would remain' (the b- prefix marks the subjunctive/optative). بدین is 'by/with this' (ب + این, 'with this'), در = 'in', and شگفت = 'wonder/marvel'; together the phrase means roughly 'if it is fitting that you remain, remain in this wonder.'
segments.s021-g73: 'چو' is a comparative conjunction here meaning 'like/as' and introduces the simile; 'شاخ درخت' is a possessive/genitive-like noun phrase 'branch of a tree' (noun + noun); 'آن دو مار سیاه' is a determiner ('آن') + numeral ('دو') + noun ('مار') with the adjective ('سیاه') following the noun, yielding 'those two black snakes.'
segments.s021-g74: 'برآمد' is a past-tense verb meaning 'rose/emerged/issued forth'; the adverbial phrase 'دگر باره' ('دگر' + 'باره') means 'again' or 'once more'; 'از کتف شاه' is a prepositional phrase ('از' + noun + possessor) meaning 'from the king's shoulder.' The verb governs the preceding simile phrase as its subject/scene.
segments.s021-g75: 'پزشکان' is the plural subject 'physicians'; 'فرزانه' is an adjective/epithet meaning 'wise' attached to them; 'گرد آمدند' is a compound verbal phrase (گرد + آمدند) meaning 'they came together' or 'they gathered' (past, 3rd plural).
segments.s021-g76: 'همه' ('all/everyone') frames the following action; 'یک به یک' is an idiom 'one by one / in turn' (literally 'one to one'); 'داستان‌ها زدند' uses the light verb 'زدند' with 'داستان‌ها' to mean 'they recited/told stories' rather than a literal 'strike' (here 'زدن' = perform/utter).
segments.s021-g77: 'ز' (poetic form of 'از') is a preposition 'from/of' used to mean 'by/with' here; 'هر گونه نیرنگ‌ها' is a quantified noun phrase 'every kind of trick(s)' ('هر' + 'گونه' + plural 'نیرنگ‌ها'); 'ساختند' is the past plural 'they devised/made' — here 'they tried or employed' such stratagems.
segments.s021-g78: 'مر' is an archaic enclitic dative pronoun used here to indicate the beneficiary of the action (i.e., 'for him' — the king); 'آن درد را' is 'that pain' with the definite/object marker 'را'; 'چاره نشناختند' literally 'they did not know a remedy' (negative past of 'شناختن'), idiomatically 'they could not find a cure.'
segments.s021-g79: 'به سان' is a fixed comparative 'like/as'; 'پزشکی' here evokes 'a physician' or 'medical art' (so 'like a physician'); 'پس' is temporal/adverbial 'then/after that'; 'ابلیس' is the subject 'Iblis (the Devil)'; 'تفت' is a past-tense verb meaning 'he burned/scorched' (here implying cauterization or a burning treatment as a false medical act). Word order places the simile before the subject and action for poetic emphasis.
segments.s021-g80: 'به فرزانگی' is an adverbial phrase formed with 'به' + abstract noun '-گی' ('with wisdom' / 'in a wise/crafty manner'); 'نزد' is the preposition 'in the presence of / to' used with a person; 'ضحاک رفت' is a simple past clause 'he went to Zahhak.' The clause as a whole means he approached Zahhak with cunning/wisdom.
segments.s021-g81: بدو گفت = بدو (to him) + گفت (said); this is the reporter: "he said to him." کاین بودنی کار بود is an older, compressed clause: ک+این = 'that this', بودنی is a verbal adjective/gerund meaning 'that which is to be' or 'destined/inevitable', کار = 'affair/matter', and بود is the copula in past (here stating that it was such). Together: 'he said to him that this was an inevitable matter.'
segments.s021-g82: بمان is the imperative 'stay/remain.' تا introduces a subordinate clause ('until/so that') and چه گردد is a subordinate interrogative/indefinite ('what will happen') with گردد in the present-subjunctive/optative third-person ('become/happen'); نباید is an impersonal negative modal ('one must not/it is not proper'), and درود is the object 'greeting' — here meaning 'do not offer greetings' or 'do not salute.'
segments.s021-g83: خورش ساز is an imperative phrase: خورش 'stew/food' plus ساز (imperative 'make/prepare' in older usage) = 'prepare food.' آرامشان ده is 'give them calm/comfort' (آرام + the 3rd-person plural clitic شان, with ده 'give' understood or attached), and به خورد ('for eating' = به + خورد) indicates purpose: 'give them comfort for eating' or 'make food and let them eat in peace.'
segments.s021-g84: نباید is the impersonal negative modal 'one should not/it must not'; جز این چاره‌ای نیز کرد literally 'do anything except this remedy' — جز introduces the sole exception, چاره‌ای is an indefinite/direct-object 'a remedy/this remedy', نیز 'also/indeed' is emphatic, and کرد is the main verb 'to do' in past infinitive sense; together: 'one should do nothing except this remedy.'
segments.s021-g85: به جز مغز مردم sets up the exception: 'except the brain(s) of the people.' مده‌شان خورش is an older-clause order where مده (here read as the imperative/giving form) with the clitic -شان 'to them' and خورش 'as food' yields 'give them as food' (in this sentence the whole construction means 'give them nothing to eat except people's brains' — the negative/exception structure is determined by the surrounding clauses). Note that شان is the 3rd-person plural object clitic attached to the verb.
segments.s021-g86: مگر introduces a proviso 'unless.' خود is emphatic/reflexive 'themselves,' بمیرند is a present-subjunctive/optative third-person plural 'they die' (used after مگر/except if), and از این پرورش 'from/through this nurturing/feeding' gives the cause or context. Together: 'unless they themselves die from this nourishment.'
segments.s021-g87: نگر is the imperative 'look!/observe!'. تا که introduces a subordinate clause ('so that/that') and links to what follows. ابلیس is the subject 'Iblis' and از این گفت‌وگوی 'from this conversation' uses از to mark origin or source ('out of/from that conversation') that frames the action to be observed.
segments.s021-g88: چه کرد و چه خواست is a double interrogative/indefinite phrase 'what he did and what he wanted' with کرد and خواست as main past-tense verbs ('did' and 'wanted'). اندر این جستجوی is a locative/instrumental phrase 'in this search/investigation' that modifies both verbs: 'what he did and sought in this search.'

sentences.s021-s13: Raise him nourished on blood like a lion, so that he may make the king brave.
sentences.s021-s14: Whatever he says he turns into an order; by his command he takes the heart captive.
sentences.s021-s15: First he gave him a dish of testicle‑yolk; thereby he kept him healthy for a time.
sentences.s021-s16: He ate, and praised him heartily; finding the taste pleasing, he named him Nīk‑bakht (the Fortunate).
sentences.s021-s17: Thus said Iblis the trickster: "Live joyfully, O haughty king."
sentences.s021-s18: That (tomorrow) I will make from that sort a stew, which will be for you wholly a nourishment.
sentences.s021-s19: He went away, and all night he was seized by anxiety, pondering what wondrous thing he should make to eat the next day.
sentences.s021-s20: They made stews from partridge and (the) white tazru, and a hopeful heart came.
sentences.s021-s25: When Zahhak thrust his hand in and ate, that alert man was astonished at it.
sentences.s021-s26: "He said to him, 'Look — tell me what you desire, O kind‑natured one.'"
sentences.s021-s27: The cook said to him, 'O king, may you always be merry and ruling (like a goat).'
sentences.s021-s28: My heart is entirely full of your love; all the provisions of my life are from your face.
sentences.s021-s1: When Iblis continually saw that speech, he cast a new evil stanza.
sentences.s021-s2: He (to him) said: 'If you turn toward me, you will obtain all the desires of your heart from the world.'
sentences.s021-s3: If you likewise make this pact, do not stray from the words and obey the command.
sentences.s021-s4: The whole world is entirely your kingdom; enemies and people and birds and fish are yours.
sentences.s021-s9: When Zahhak heard, he struck him, and for his food he made a place for him.
sentences.s021-s10: By the ruler's command he gave him the key of the royal kitchen.
sentences.s021-s11: At that time provision was not plentiful, so that the cook made stews out of the poorer of the edibles.
sentences.s021-s12: From every kind of meat—from birds and from quadrupeds—the cook would bring (or make and bring) stews one by one.
sentences.s021-s5: When this had been done, he took up another device; he devised one solution — a wonder among wonders.
sentences.s021-s6: A youth arrayed himself, eloquent, clear-sighted in heart and a counselor.
sentences.s021-s7: Hamidun turned his face to Zahhak; he had nothing except praise and words.
sentences.s021-s8: He said to him: 'If I am acceptable to the king, I will (be) one renowned and pure خوالیگرم.'
sentences.s021-s45: Unless someone secretly devised a remedy so that it might be made/known among the people of the world.
sentences.s021-s21: When the Arab king laid hands on the bread, he entrusted the seal to the small‑minded chief.
sentences.s021-s22: On the third day he set the table with birds and lambs, arranging everything entirely in various kinds.
sentences.s021-s23: On the fourth day, when he set the table, he made a stew from the back of a young ox.
sentences.s021-s24: Into it he put saffron and rosewater, and likewise aged wine and pure musk.
sentences.s021-s29: I have a certain need to be near the king, and although I do not have that standing.
sentences.s021-s30: that he should issue an order so that I may kiss the top of his shoulder and place my eyes and face upon him.
sentences.s021-s31: When Zahhak heard his speech, he did not perceive his secret dealings.
sentences.s021-s32: He said to him, 'I have this desire for you; loftiness will be gained from this name of yours.'
sentences.s021-s33: He commanded that the demon, like his mate, should kiss him, giving a kiss upon his firm chest.
sentences.s021-s34: He kissed (it) and then vanished to the ground; no one in the world had seen this marvel.
sentences.s021-s35: Two black snakes sprang from his two shoulders; sorrow came upon him, and he sought a remedy from every direction.
sentences.s021-s36: At last they cut both off from his (place); if it befit (you) to remain, (do so) in this wonder.
sentences.s021-s37: Like a tree branch, the two black snakes again emerged from the king's shoulder.
sentences.s021-s38: The wise physicians gathered round; each, one by one, recounted stories.
sentences.s021-s39: They tried every kind of stratagem, yet they could not find a remedy for that ailment (for him).
sentences.s021-s40: Then Iblis, like a physician, applied burning (cautery); with sagacity he went to Zahhak.
sentences.s021-s41: He said to him, "This is a destined affair; remain and see what will happen — do not offer greetings."
sentences.s021-s42: Prepare food and give them comfort to eat; nothing should be done except this one remedy.
sentences.s021-s43: Give them nothing to eat except the brains of the people, unless they themselves die from this nourishment.
sentences.s021-s44: Watch (observe) what Iblis did and what he sought in this inquiry arising from that conversation.
